Organic Chemistry I¶

Key Norms by A. G. Hossion¶

Lesson-01 to 03¶

Lesson-01_ch1&2¶

⏣ How to read and/or draw bond line and condensed structure of Organic compounds, Functional Groups (FG)¶

  • Bond-Line Drawings : How to read and draw Bond-Line/Condensed structure Drawings?
  • FG: Identification and naming Functional groups of organic compounds - alkane, alkene, alkyne, alkyl halide, alcohol, ether, thiol, aromatic or arene, ketone, aldehyde, carboxylic acid, acyl halide, anhydride, ester, amide and amine.

🍓🍓 Concept Check and/or Mastering the Modality¶

  • Identify the number of carbon atoms and hydrogen atoms in the compounds below, draw condensed or skeletal/line structure:

  • identify and label all functional groups in these two compounds:

Lesson-02_ch1¶

⏣ Hybrid Atomic Orbitals (AOs), Molecular Orbitals (MOs)¶

  • Hybrid atomic orbitals: Know sp, sp2, sp3 hybrid orbitals. Understand how sigma bond, pi bond, carbon-carbon double bond, carbon-carbon triple bond are form. Know unshared pairs of electron hybridization for oxygen and nitrogen containing compounds. and Steric number.

🍓🍓Concept Check and/or Mastering the Modality¶

  • Identify the hybridization state and geometry of each of the carbon, nitrogen and oxygen atoms in following structures, Identify the location of all lone pairs in these compound if not provided:

  • Rank the indicated bonds in terms of increasing bond length, and Count the total number of Sigma (σ) bonds and Phi(π)bonds in the compounds:

Lesson-03_ch1¶

⏣ Polar and non-Polar Compounds, Dipole moment(µ)¶

  • Polar and non-polar covalent bond : Know the difference covalent bond vs polar covalent bond. Get familiar to identify partial charges vs complete charges.
  • Dipole moment: What is dipole moment(µ)? Learn how to calculate and draw dipole moment (sign) for a bond (C-Cl, etc) in compounds. Predict molecular geometry on the basis of VSEPR theory. Know how to identify whether compound can exhibit dipole moment and compound is polar or non-polar.
  • Intermolecular forces and physical properties - m.p., b.p., solubility: What are dipole-dipole interaction, hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces and/or fleeting dipole-dipole interactions? And understand how these properties change the physical properties of melting point/ boiling point/ Solubility of compounds. Protic/aprotic solvent.

🍓🍓Concept Check and/or Mastering the Modality¶

  • Identify whether following molecular exhibit dipole moment or polar covalent bonds. If so, indicate the direction of the net molecular dipole mement.

    H2O , CH4O

  • Arrange the following compounds in order of increasing boiling point:

  • which of the following protic and aprotic solvent, explain:

Lesson-04¶

Lesson-04_ch02¶

⏣ Resoanace structure and Curved arrow drawing¶

  • Formal Charge : Understand how to identify formal charges in the structure. Know how to calculate formal charges of atoms in the structure.
  • Formal charge on Carbon: Carbon atoms with formal charges, know Carbocation Vs Carboanion and understand how to identify lone pairs on oxygen and nitrogen atoms.
  • Curved arrow and resonance structure: understand the five patterns, how to draw right arrow to valid correct resonance structure. (i) Formal charges in resonance structure - allylic (ii) Drawing resonance structure via pattern recognition
  • What is resonance hybrid and understand delocalized and localized lone pairs.

🍓🍓Concept Check and/or Mastering the Modality¶

  • For each of the following carbocation and/or carboanion 'C' identify number of hydrogen, then use that information to determine hybridization state.


  • For each of the following, draw all missing lone pairs or formal charge:


  • for each compound below, identify all lone pairs and indicate whether each lone pair is localized or delocalized. Then, use that information (or formal charge) to draw the cuved arrow, all significant resonance and hybrid structures.


Lesson-05¶

Lesson-05_ch03¶

⏣ Acids and Bases¶

  • Acids and Bases: Know acids and bases definition on the basis of Bronsted-Lowry concept and Lewis concept. Understand how to compare the strength of acids and bases on the basis of structure of molecules (in absence of pKa or pKb values).
  • Lewis Acids and Bases: Be able to justify Lewis concept is the most fundamental of the acid-base concepts, and the most general; it includes all the other concepts such as Bronsted-Lowry concept. Lewis concept of acidity and basicity fundamental to our understanding of Organic Chemistry.
  • Curved arrow and electron density: Know how to draw curved arrow to show the flow of electron density and transferring proton.
  • Acidity on the basis of Bronsted-Lowry and Lewis Acids-Bases Concepts:

    Quantitative Perspective: [pKa and proton transfer ability] Understand how to use pKa to compare acidity and basicity.
    Qualitative Perspective: Be able to understand the factors such as charge on atoms, resonance, induction, sp/sp2/sp3 orbitals affect the stability of conjugate base. Understand how to compare the strength of acids and bases on the basis of structure of molecules (in absence of pKa or pKb values).
    Be able to use both qualitative and quantitative perspectives to predict the position of equilibrium in reaction. Organic acid-base mechanism.

  • Leveling and solvent effects and counter-ions: Understand the strongest base and the strongest acid can be present in any particular solvent such as in a basic solution and/or in an acidic solution.

🍓🍓Concept Check and/or Mastering the Modality¶
